one_line_takeaway: Using an LLM to infer taint source/sink specifications and to triage CodeQL's paths roughly doubles real-world Java vulnerability detection (27 to 55 of 120) while lowering false discovery rate, though absolute recall stays below 50% and OS-command-injection remains out of reach.
key_finding: IRIS with GPT-4 detects 55 of 120 vulnerabilities (45.83%) versus 27 (22.50%) for CodeQL alone -- a +28 (103.7%) increase -- while simultaneously lowering average FDR from 90.03% to 84.82% (a 5.21-point drop) and roughly doubling AvgF1 from 0.076 to 0.177. The detection gain is thus not bought with extra false positives. Ablations show both LLM-inferred sources and sinks are needed: replacing either with CodeQL's specs collapses detection back toward baseline (SrcGPT4+SnkQL=36, SrcQL+SnkGPT4=24, SrcQL+SnkQL=27 vs full 55). IRIS also surfaced 4 previously unknown vulnerabilities (3 CWE-22, 1 CWE-94, incl. a Zip-Slip in alluxio 2.9.4) on latest versions of 30 projects that CodeQL missed.
core_contribution: IRIS is a neuro-symbolic system for whole-repository security vulnerability detection that combines LLMs with static taint analysis (CodeQL). The paper's own novelty is threefold: (1) using an LLM to automatically infer CWE-specific taint source/sink specifications for both external library APIs (few-shot, ~3-shot) and internal public function parameters (zero-shot, augmented with README/JavaDoc), replacing hand-written specs; (2) feeding those inferred specs into a specialized CodeQL query per vulnerability to find unsanitized source-to-sink paths; and (3) an LLM-based contextual triage step (FilterPath) that classifies each reported path as true/false positive using code context along the path, cutting false alarms. Alongside the method, the authors contribute CWE-Bench-Java, a new curated benchmark of 120 manually validated real-world Java vulnerabilities. This is distinct from cited prior spec-inference work (MERLIN, Seldon, InspectJS) which uses probabilistic/optimization modeling over library code rather than LLM inference, and from prior learning-based detectors (GNN/LSTM/Transformer) which do only binary method-level classification.
threat_model: The security task addressed is detection of taint-style injection vulnerabilities in real-world Java code: an attacker supplies untrusted input (e.g., an HttpServletRequest path, a user-supplied string, a checkpoint Zip entry name) that flows unsanitized from a source to a dangerous sink. The four CWE classes targeted are CWE-22 (Path Traversal / Zip-Slip), CWE-78 (OS Command Injection), CWE-79 (Cross-Site Scripting), and CWE-94 (Code Injection, e.g., Java EL expression execution enabling arbitrary code such as Runtime.exec). IRIS is a defensive detection tool, not an attack; the 'adversary' is the classic untrusted-data-reaching-sink model of static taint analysis.
limitations: Reported AvgFDR is only an upper bound because the metric labels any alarm not hitting a known patched location as a false positive; a manual re-check of 50 GPT-4 alarms found 27 with plausible attack surfaces, giving a refined ~46% FDR, so headline FDR overstates real imprecision. CWE-78 (OS Command Injection) stayed hard for all LLMs (GPT-4 only 3/13) because such bugs involve gadget-chains or file-write side effects that static analysis tracks poorly -- a fundamental static-analysis limitation the authors defer to dynamic analysis. Contextual-analysis benefit depends on model reasoning: only GPT-4, GPT-3.5, and Llama-3 70B gained precision/F1 from it, while smaller models (which over-predict 'vulnerable') were hurt. Scope is limited to 4 CWE classes, Java/Maven libraries only, one vulnerability per project, and reliance on CodeQL for the data-flow engine and candidate extraction. Absolute recall is still under 50%, so most vulnerabilities remain undetected.

datasets_used: CWE-Bench-Java (new, curated by the authors): 120 manually validated vulnerabilities, one per real-world Java/Maven project, across CWE-22 (55), CWE-78 (13), CWE-79 (31), CWE-94 (21); avg ~300K SLOC per project, max ~7M SLOC, 10 projects >1M LOC; curated by filtering GitHub Advisory DB (1065 CVEs) down through libraries.io (430), snyk.io cross-validation (265), buildable (149), manual checks (120)
quantitative_results: 4 previously unknown vulnerabilities found on 30 latest-version projects (3 CWE-22, 1 CWE-94; 16 projects raised >=1 alert)
Refined manual FDR: 27/50 sampled GPT-4 alarms had plausible attack surface => ~46% estimated FDR (vs 84.82% upper bound)
LLM sink-spec recall vs CodeQL specs: GPT-4 highest at 87.11%; GPT-4 source-spec precision >70% (manual over 960 sampled labels)
Per-CWE IRIS+GPT-4 detections: CWE-22 31/55, CWE-78 3/13, CWE-79 13/31, CWE-94 8/21
Ablation (#Detected, GPT-4): SrcGPT4+SnkGPT4=55; SrcGPT4+SnkQL=36; SrcQL+SnkQL=27; SrcQL+SnkGPT4=24
baselines_compared: CodeQL v2.15.3 with built-in per-CWE Security queries (primary baseline, 27/120)
